16.display: A rectangle, 4 cm wide by 2 cm tall

17.display: A square (with diagonals to the apex sometimes shown)

23.display: Correct — a cube has identical square faces on every side, so its plan, front elevation, and side elevation are all the same square.

24.display: False — the front and side elevations of a cuboid are only identical if the cuboid has a square cross-section from those directions; otherwise they show different-sized rectangles.

25.display: The plan view would be a row of 3 squares (a long rectangle); the front elevation (viewed along the row) would show just 1 square, since the cubes line up directly behind one another.

26.display: A cuboid — a solid where the plan, front elevation, and side elevation are all rectangles of different sizes is most likely a cuboid, since each view corresponds to one pair of matching rectangular faces.

27.display: Always — many different solids (e.g. a cylinder and a cone standing upright) can share the same circular plan view but look different from the front.

28.display: Example: a cylinder and a cone standing upright both have a circular plan view, but their front elevations are different (a rectangle for the cylinder, a triangle for the cone) — so the plan alone cannot tell them apart.

29.display: The plan view is a circle (the base, often shown with the apex marked as a dot in the centre); the front elevation is a triangle, because the cone's height and slanted sides are seen from the side.

30.display: True when viewed from the triangular end (showing the cross-section); if viewed from the rectangular side, the front elevation would be a rectangle instead.

31.display: Plan: a single square; front elevation: a tall thin rectangle 1 cube wide and 4 cubes high; side elevation: the same tall thin rectangle.

32.display: A wide rectangle (6 cm by 2 cm) for the large cuboid with a narrower, taller rectangle (2 cm by 3 cm) centred on top of it, giving a stepped outline.

33.display: A staircase-shaped outline of 3 squares, each one step up and one step back from the last, forming a zig-zag profile.

34.display: Plan: a circle of radius 3 cm; front elevation: a rectangle 6 cm wide (the diameter) by 8 cm tall (the height).

35.display: A rectangle (the cuboid part of the house) with a triangle sitting on top of it (the roof).

36.display: A cylinder — a circular plan with matching rectangular front and side elevations of the same size is the plan/elevation set of a cylinder.

37.display: A triangular prism lying on one of its rectangular faces — its plan is a rectangle, and both its front and side elevations can be triangles depending on the direction viewed.

38.display: Use the plan to find the base's length and width, and use the front or side elevation to find the height; multiply length × width × height to find the volume.

39.display: The cube's front elevation is a square (constant width all the way up), while the pyramid's front elevation is a triangle (the sides slope inward to a point at the top).

40.display: Your own combined-cuboid solid with clearly described plan, front elevation, and side elevation, including measurements.

41.display: L-shape of two squares from above
